16: homologoumenos mega estin to tes eusebeias mysterion: 'Great indeed, as we confess, is the mystery of godliness.' What follows (vv. 16b-c) is almost certainly a pre-Pauline hymn or creedal fragment in six lines, possibly arranged in two strophes of three: (1) manifested in flesh / vindicated by Spirit / seen by angels; (2) proclaimed among nations / believed in the world / taken up in glory. The six verbs are all aorist passives — divine actions done to Christ. The structure mirrors Phil 2:6-11's humiliation-exaltation pattern: the first triad (incarnation, vindication, heavenly witness) parallels the descending and ascending of the Christ-hymn.
1: pistos ho logos: ei tis episkopēs oregetai kalou ergou epithumei — 'trustworthy is the saying: if anyone aspires to oversight, he desires a good work.' The second pistos ho logos formula introduces the overseer-standard as a recognized community principle. Episkopē (oversight, the office of overseer) — the noun from which episkopos comes; the office, not merely the function. Oregetai (aspire, reach after) and epithumei (desire earnestly) are both present tenses: the desire for the office is ongoing and appropriate. The key phrase: it is a kalon ergon (good work/noble task) — the honor of the office is inseparable from its labor.
2: dei oun ton episkopon anepilēmpton einai — 'the overseer must therefore be beyond reproach.' Anepilēmptos (beyond reproach, without basis for accusation) — not sinless but without a sustained vulnerable point that opponents can attack. The qualification list that follows is not primarily doctrinal but character-based: the overseer's fitness is demonstrated by their life before appointment. Mias gynaikos andra (husband of one wife, lit. 'a one-woman man') — the phrase is much debated: prohibition of polygamy? Of remarriage after divorce? Of remarriage after a spouse's death? Most likely a reputation for marital fidelity and moral consistency rather than a specific marital history requirement.
3: mē paroinon mē plēktēn alla epieikē amachon aphilarguron — 'not a drunkard, not violent, but gentle, peaceable, not a lover of money.' The series of negatives and positives are character-virtues recognizable in both Jewish and Hellenistic leadership ethics: epieikēs (gentle, reasonable, equitable — a Hellenistic virtue term, Phil 4:5) and amachos (not contentious, peaceable) contrast with the violence and aggression that disqualified. Aphilarguros (not a lover of money, free from avarice) — the philarguros (lover of money) is identified in 6:10 as the root of all evils: the overseer must be structurally free from the vice that corrupts leadership.
4: tou idiou oikou kalōs proistamenon — 'managing his own household well.' Proistēmi (to stand before, to lead, to manage) is the same word used for community leaders in 1 Thess 5:12. The analogy is household → church: competence in leading the smaller unit qualifies (and failing to lead the smaller disqualifies) for leading the larger. Tekna echonta en hypotagē meta pasēs semnotētos (having children in submission with all dignity) — the children's submission is evidence of the father's leadership competence, not merely their own virtue. The father takes responsibility for the community character of his household.
5: ei de tis tou idiou oikou proistasthai ouk oiden, pōs ekklēsias theou epimelēsetai — 'if someone does not know how to manage his own household, how will he care for God's church?' The a minori ad maius argument (from lesser to greater): the church is harder to lead than a household, so household leadership competence is the minimum qualification. Epimelēsetai (will care for/attend to) shifts the metaphor from management to pastoral care — attending to needs, nurturing. The church is 'God's household' (oikos theou, 3:15), so the qualification for God's house must exceed what qualifies for a human one.
6: mē neophyton, hina mē tuphotheIs krima empesē tou diabolou — 'not a recent convert, lest being puffed up he fall into the condemnation of the devil.' Neophutos (newly planted, new convert) — a horticultural metaphor for someone recently planted in the faith, lacking deep root. Tuphōtheis (being puffed up, inflated with conceit) — the participle of tuphoō, used of smoke or vapor that inflates without substance. The genitive 'of the devil' (tou diabolou) in 'condemnation of the devil' may mean: the same judgment that the devil received (the devil's own condemnation) or the judgment the devil brings to puffed-up leaders.
7: dei de auton kai martyrian kalēn echein apo tōn exōthen — 'he must also have a good testimony from outsiders.' Exōthen (from outside — those not in the community) — the overseer's reputation must survive scrutiny from people who have no interest in favorably evaluating him. The twofold danger in v.7 — 'fall into disgrace' (oneidismon, reproach) and 'the devil's trap' (pagida tou diabolou) — uses the same pagida (snare/trap) that 6:9 applies to people who desire to be rich: the devil sets structural traps through social vulnerabilities.
8: diakonous hōsautōs semnous — 'deacons likewise must be dignified.' Semnous (dignified, serious, honorable) — the quality of gravitas appropriate to someone in public service. The deacon-list parallels the overseer-list with some differences: no requirement for ability to teach (since deacons were not primarily teaching roles), but addition of 'dilogous' (double-tongued/saying different things to different people) as a specific vice to avoid — relevant to deacons who served as messengers and household-visitors in the early church. Oinō pollō prosechontas (not given to much wine) — the comparative suggests the concern is excess, not total abstinence.
9: echontas to mystērion tēs pisteōs en kathara suneidēsei — 'holding the mystery of the faith with a clear conscience.' To mystērion tēs pisteōs (the mystery of the faith) — the revealed content of Christian belief, paralleling the 'mystery of godliness' in v.16. The deacon must hold this doctrinal deposit, but the manner of holding it — en kathara suneidēsei (in a clean conscience) — ensures that knowledge and moral character remain integrated. A deacon who has the doctrinal knowledge but a defiled conscience is disqualified by the same logic as the false teachers of ch.1 who have deviated from a clean heart and good conscience.
10: kai houtoi de dokimazesthōsan prōton, eita diakoneitōsan anenklētoi ontes — 'let them also be tested first; then let them serve as deacons if they are blameless.' Dokimazesthōsan (let them be tested, proven) — the present passive imperative signals an ongoing evaluation process before formal appointment. Prōton (first) distinguishes deacon appointment from overseer appointment: there is a testing phase between candidacy and office. Anenklētoi (without accusation, blameless — used in Titus 1:6 for elders) — the same accountability standard as the overseer applies.
11: gynaikas hōsautōs semnas mē diabolous nēphalious pistas en pasin — 'women likewise must be dignified, not slanderers, sober-minded, faithful in all things.' The referent is disputed: wives of deacons or a separate order of women deacons (female deacons)? The parallel structure (hōsautōs — likewise — introduces the women after both the overseer and deacon paragraphs) and the character-list format (paralleling the deacon-list) suggests these are women serving a diaconal function. The specific vice 'diabolous' (slanderers — lit. 'devils,' the same word as 'the devil') was a known risk for those doing pastoral house-to-house visitation: gossip and slander destroy the trust such ministry requires.
12: diakonoi estōsan mias gynaikos andres tōn idiōn teknōn kalōs proistamenoi kai tōn idiōn oikōn — 'let deacons be husbands of one wife, managing their children and their own households well.' The same mias gynaikos andra (one-woman man) requirement as for overseers; the same household-management criterion. The parallelism between overseer and deacon qualifications at this point suggests both are full-time ministry roles with structural accountability, not merely volunteer positions.
13: hoi gar kalōs diakonēsantes bathmon heautois kalon peripoiountai kai pollēn parrēsian en pistei tē en Christō Iēsou — 'for those who serve well as deacons gain a good standing for themselves and great boldness in the faith that is in Christ Jesus.' Bathmon kalon (good standing — lit. 'a good step/grade') — the metaphor of a step suggests advancement, recognition, perhaps eventual movement to overseer-level leadership. Parrēsia (boldness, frank speech, confidence) — the freedom of speech that belongs to those with nothing to hide: the deacon who has served faithfully can speak with authority in the community.
14: tauta soi graphō elpizōn elthein pros se en tachei — 'I write these things to you, hoping to come to you soon.' The letter situates itself as a substitute for Paul's delayed presence: the written instructions must serve in place of the apostle's personal direction. En tachei (soon, quickly) — Paul's hope of rapid return; the conditional 'if I am delayed' (v.15) acknowledges uncertainty. The letter's governing purpose is stated in v.15: so that Timothy will know how people ought to behave in God's household — the instructions are not personal advice but community-ordering guidance.
15: stulos kai hedraiōma tēs alētheias — 'pillar and foundation of the truth.' These two images apply to the church as the community that upholds and undergirds truth. Stulos (pillar — a weight-bearing column, as in the pillars of a temple) supports the truth from below; hedraiōma (buttress, foundation, firm base) stabilizes it. The metaphors answer the false teachers: when the community's teaching authority is properly ordered, the truth stands; when it is disordered, the truth is threatened. The church does not generate truth but holds and displays it.
